title: The Desire for God
scripture: Ps 42:2-3; Acts 17:26-28; St. Augustine, Confessions I,1
html:
Big Question

Why, deep in our heart, are we never completely satisfied?

It was the night of November 23, 1654, and Blaise Pascal —one of the greatest geniuses humanity has ever produced: mathematician, physicist, inventor of the first calculator, father of probability theory— was about to die. Not his body, but his soul as he had known it. That night, during hours of ecstasy that he called his «night of fire,» Pascal found something that all his intelligence had been unable to give him.

When the experience ended, he took a pen and wrote on a parchment words that he sewed into the lining of his coat and that no one found until after his death: «FIRE. God of Abraham, God of Isaac, God of Jacob, not of the philosophers and scholars. Certainty, certainty, feeling, joy, peace. God of Jesus Christ... Forgetting of the world and of everything except God.»

Pascal, who had scaled the heights of human knowledge, discovered something he would later write in his Pensées: «There is a God-shaped abyss in the heart of man, and only God can fill it.» He did not say this theoretically. He had lived it. He had tried to fill that abyss with science, with fame, with philosophy —and nothing fit. Only God.

The Catechism of the Catholic Church confirms this discovery in its first substantive paragraphs: the desire for God is written in the human heart (CCC 27). It is not something we learn; it is something we are. No matter how much we try to fill our lives with achievements, relationships, pleasures, or securities, there will always be a space that only the Infinite can occupy. Saint Augustine expressed it best: «You have made us for yourself, O Lord, and our heart is restless until it rests in you.»

This lesson is the first in the entire Catechism because it touches on the fundamental question: Why does religion exist? Why has the human being, in all cultures and ages, sought God? The Catechism's answer is bold: because God sought us first.

Activity

Imagine you are very, very thirsty. You drink water, but soon you are thirsty again. You drink juice, but it doesn't quite satisfy you either. That thirst that never ends is like the desire for God in our heart. God made us for Himself, and no matter how many good things we have, we will always want more until we are with Him. St. Augustine discovered this after searching for happiness in many places. In the end, he understood that only God fills the heart.

Draw a heart and inside write or draw the things you like most. Next to it, draw Jesus. Ask yourself: Does any of these things make you happy forever, or only for a while?

1. Man is «capable» of God

The Catechism opens its first section with a statement that should not escape our notice: man is «capable» of God (CCC 27, chapter title). The word is important. It does not say that man «wants» God, or that he «needs» God — though both are true. It says that he is capable, that is, there exists in his being an openness, an orientation, a real capacity to know and love his Creator.

This capacity is radically different from a dog's capacity to understand its owner or a computer's capacity to process data. It is a capacity of personal order: we are made for a relationship with Someone, not for a system of beliefs. That is why the Catechism says that «man's dignity rests above all on the fact that he is called to communion with God» (CCC 27).

Two modern heresies

To understand this point well, it is worth pointing out two opposite errors that circulate widely:

First: the one that says religion is a psychological projection, an illusion that man invents because he cannot bear reality. This idea (popularized by Freud and Feuerbach) holds that God is a human invention. The Catechism responds: if man seeks God, it is because God created him for Himself. The capacity to seek presupposes the reality of what is sought, just as thirst presupposes water.

Second: the one that says faith is a blind, irrational leap, a matter of «feelings» that has nothing to do with reason. This idea is equally harmful because it reduces faith to an arbitrary act. The Catechism, following St. Thomas Aquinas and the First Vatican Council, affirms that man can arrive at the knowledge of God with the natural light of reason (CCC 31-36). Faith is not irrational; it is suprarational.

Context

Perhaps you come to this class unsure of whether you believe in God. Welcome. This space is for you too. The Catechism is not addressed only to those who already believe, but to every person of good will. St. John Paul II, at the beginning of his pontificate, said: «Open the doors to Christ!» He did not say «close the doors to those who doubt.» Faith is a dialogue: God speaks, man responds. And sometimes the response begins with an honest question. Yours has already begun.

You do not need to know everything to start. You need to be willing to ask. The Catechism offers you answers, but answers only make sense if you have first felt the question.

2. The traces of desire in history

The Catechism points out an indisputable fact: «in many ways, throughout history to the present day, men have expressed their search for God» (CCC 28). And it mentions prayers, sacrifices, rituals, meditations. The religious phenomenon is universal. There is no human culture that has not had some form of religion.

St. Paul, in the Areopagus of Athens, said it in a way that the Catechism captures:

«From one ancestor he made all nations to inhabit the whole earth... that they would search for God and perhaps grope for him and find him — though indeed he is not far from each one of us, for in him we live and move and have our being.» (Acts 17:26-28)

Note the expression: «grope for him.» Like someone walking in darkness, extending their hands. The world's religions are those extended hands. The Catechism does not despise non-Christian religions; it sees them as authentic — though imperfect — expressions of that universal desire for God. The fullness of the response, however, is Jesus Christ.

The concrete forms of desire

The desire for God is not only expressed in explicit worship. It also manifests in:

  • The search for truth: «Does my life have meaning?» «Is there something after death?»
  • The yearning for beauty: a symphony, a sunset, a work of art that overwhelms us.
  • The thirst for justice: indignation at innocent suffering.
  • The desire for unconditional love: being loved for who I am, not for what I give.
  • The experience of wonder: looking at the stars and feeling that something surpasses us.

All these yearnings are not system failures. They are echoes. Signals of a call that comes from beyond us, but resonates within.

Connection

In a world that tells you happiness is in the next video, the next purchase, the next relationship, the Catechism tells you something radically different: you will never be completely satisfied. And that is not bad. That dissatisfaction is your soul asking for more. Do not anesthetize it with screens. Ask yourself: What is that thing I really want? If you could ask for just one thing, something that would make you truly happy, forever... what would it be?

The young saints (St. Thérèse of Lisieux, St. Francis of Assisi, Blessed Carlo Acutis) understood this. They did not despise the good things of the world, but they knew that none of them was the final answer. That is why they were free.

3. What prevents us from recognizing it?

If the desire for God is universal, why doesn't everyone recognize it? The Catechism is honest in pointing out the obstacles (CCC 29):

  1. Rebellion against evil. «If God exists, why does He allow suffering?» This question, the most painful of all, can lead to rejecting God not from unbelief but from indignation. It is an obstacle that deserves respect, not an easy argument.
  2. Religious ignorance. Many do not know the faith because it was never taught to them, or because what they were taught was a caricature.
  3. Indifference. Perhaps the most widespread obstacle in our societies. It is not that people are against God; it is that God simply does not appear on their radar. Concerns, worries, constant entertainment fill the space where the question of God once dwelt.
  4. The riches and cares of the world. Jesus himself warned: the seed of the word can be choked by «the cares of life and the deceitfulness of riches» (Mk 4:19). It is not that having things is bad; it is that things can occupy so much space that they leave no room for what is essential.
  5. The bad example of believers. Schizophrenia between faith and life, scandals, hypocrisy. Christ never promised his followers would be perfect, but the scandal of bad Christians is real and harmful.
  6. Hostile currents of thought. Materialism, relativism, reductionist scientism that declares only what can be measured is real.
  7. Sin. At bottom, the sinful man «hides from God out of fear and flees from his call» (CCC 29). Like Adam in Eden.

But the good news is that God never gives up. Paragraph 30 of the Catechism is a declaration of hope: although man can forget God or reject Him, He never ceases to call every man to seek Him and find life and happiness.

Activity

Of the seven obstacles, which do you think is most common among the people you know? Talk about it with someone you trust or write it in a notebook. It is not about judging, but about understanding.

4. Connection with Scripture

The Old Testament expresses this desire with an image that never ages:

As the deer longs for flowing streams,
so my soul longs for you, O God.
My soul thirsts for God, for the living God:
When shall I come and behold the face of God?

— Psalm 42:1-2

The psalmist uses the most powerful image he could find: a deer panting with thirst in the desert, about to die, seeking water desperately. It is not a soft or decorative desire. It is a vital necessity. This is our soul without God.

And the New Testament picks up this same image but adds a certainty: God is not a distant destination we reach after a long search. St. Paul says that in Him «we live and move and have our being» (Acts 17:28). It is not that we seek an absent God; it is that we are already in Him, like fish in water. Only sometimes we do not know it.

The YouCat, the youth catechism that follows the structure of the CCC, begins its first question exactly here: «Why is man capable of God?» (YouCat 1). And answers: «Because God, who created him out of love, created him for love.» There is no separation between what we are and to whom we belong.

5. God comes to meet us

The second part of the chapter («The ways of coming to know God», CCC 31-35) develops how man can, with reason alone, come to know God's existence. But the Catechism does not stop there. It immediately adds that God, besides being knowable by reason, has come out to meet man through Revelation (CCC 50 ff.).

This is crucial: the Christian faith is not the result of human effort to reach God, but the response to a divine initiative. God seeks us first. «You would not be seeking me if you had not already found me,» said Pascal. Or, as the Catechism writes citing Augustine:

«You yourself encourage him to delight in your praise, because you have made us for yourself, and our heart is restless until it rests in you.» (CCC 30)

St. Augustine ends his Confessions with a prayer that anticipates heaven:

«When I am completely united to you, there will be no more sorrows or trials; completely filled with you, my life will be complete.» Confessions 10:28,39 — cited in CCC 45

Going Deeper

The natural desire for God has been a topic of theological debate for centuries. The Thomistic tradition distinguishes between a natural desire to see God (desiderium naturale videndi Deum) and obediential capacity (potentia oboedientialis) by which human nature can be elevated by grace. St. Thomas (STh I, q.12, a.1) holds that there is in man a natural desire to know the first cause, but the beatific vision surpasses all natural capacity and is pure gift of grace.

Hans Urs von Balthasar, in The Heart of the World, develops this tension poetically: man is like a bow stretched between time and eternity. The arrow of desire points to God, but only God can release the string. This is the nucleus of Christian thought on grace: it does not nullify nature, it perfects it.

Recommended reading: Joseph Ratzinger, Introduction to Christianity, chapter 1. Also YouCat 1-4, which offers an accessible version of the same content.

For Reflection

1. If you could have just one thing that would make you completely and permanently happy, what would it be?

Take five minutes to answer honestly. Don't give the «correct» answer. Then ask yourself: if you achieved it, would your heart be at rest forever?

2. Have you ever felt that «something more» that you can't quite identify?

Sometimes it manifests as objectless nostalgia, as an inexplicable sadness in the midst of joy, or as that moment when something beautiful overwhelms you and you feel it points to something even greater.

3. Of the seven obstacles to recognizing God that the Catechism points out, which do you recognize most in your own life?

It is not about judging yourself. It is about knowing yourself. Because the path to God starts where you are, not where you should be.

4. Is there anyone in your life who, through their example or words, has helped you «seek God»?

The Catechism says this search requires «the witness of others who teach him to seek God» (CCC 30). Who has been that witness for you?

Key Takeaway

The desire for God is not something we should repress or overcome. It is the truest part of us. It is the compass that, if we don't turn it off with noise, points us toward our origin and our destiny.

Cultural Resources

Film The Tree of Life (Terrence Malick, 2011). The final beach scene is a visual meditation on the desire for eternity. Malick, a former philosopher, builds the entire film as a question about suffering and beauty that only finds an answer in hope.

Literature Fulton J. Sheen, Peace of Soul and Way to Happiness. Sheen was one of the great Catholic communicators of the 20th century. His books are gems of practical wisdom, full of stories and anecdotes that illuminate profound truths. The chapter «Our Needs and Their Fulfillment» in Guide to Contentment develops precisely the theme of the God-shaped hole. Also St. Augustine, Confessions, Book I.

Youth Catechism YouCat (Youth Catechism), questions 1-4. YouCat is the Catechism in youth language, approved by Benedict XVI. Each question comes with a biblical citation, an icon, and a prayer. Available free at youcat.org and as an app. An excellent complementary resource for this lesson.

Opus Dei The series «Themes of the Christian Faith» from Opus Dei (opusdei.org) offers brief articles on the Catechism. Theme 1, «The Longing for God», develops paragraphs 27-30 in depth. They also have Synthesis of the Catholic Faith by Javier Yániz, available as a free PDF.

Music Hakuna Group Music is a phenomenon of contemporary Catholic music that fills stadiums in Spain and Latin America. Their songs like «Solo Tú» or «Madre» express the desire for God in youthful language. Also Mendelssohn's «Psalm 42» sets precisely the text we have meditated to music.

Art The Creation of Adam (Michelangelo, Sistine Chapel). The image of God extending His hand toward Adam visually captures the theme of this lesson: God seeks us, calls us, desires us. Desire is not unilateral; it is a dialogue initiated by God.

Audiovisual Catechesis Real + True (osvnews.com/real-true) is a multimedia project of the Catechism. Their episode on paragraphs 27-30 is titled «Made for More». The channel Ascension Presents with Fr. Mike Schmitz has accessible and profound videos. In Spanish, Infinito + 1 by Juan Manuel Cotelo offers the series «Catequizis».
